intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A deploy-only command-line tool that scaffolds, validates, bundles, and upserts a Managed Deep Agents project onto the LangSmith platform. It is a deterministic tool with no LLM inference of its own: it reads a project's declared sources (`agent.json`, `AGENTS.md`, `tools.json`, `skills/`, `subagents/`), assembles the payload and managed-directory tree, and ships it over HTTPS with the developer's own `LANGSMITH_API_KEY`. It exposes four subcommands (`init`, `deploy`, `agents`, `mcp-servers`), must validate that every referenced MCP server is already registered and invocable (never auto-registering), and must gate remote-agent updates and resource deletions behind explicit confirmation.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python CLI (`deepagents-cli` 0.2.2) built on `argparse`, dispatching from `main.py` into a `deploy/` package: `project.py` (parse + validate on-disk project), `payload.py` (pure-function bundle assembly), `api_client.py` (thin `httpx` client, `trust_env=False`), `mcp_resolver.py` (workspace MCP resolution), `state.py` (user-local deploy state), and `commands.py` (the four subcommand handlers). The in-scope surface performs only file reads and HTTPS calls — no subprocess, shell, or model client. A root `.mcp.json` registers two remote HTTPS docs MCP servers for the repo's own dev-time agent. The shipped `THREAT_MODEL.md` is stale (pre-0.1.0 split) and its threat table describes the interactive-REPL surfaces that have since moved to the out-of-scope `libs/code` package.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a mature, security-conscious deterministic bundler whose transport-integrity guarantees are enforced unevenly: the platform endpoint is rigorously locked to HTTPS (no userinfo, `trust_env=False`, proxy env stripped) and the bundle is tightly contained (symlink and path-escape rejection, pure-function assembly from declared sources only), yet the parallel MCP-server trust path never validates the scheme of an MCP URL — `mcp-servers add`/`update` and the deploy resolver accept `http://` / `ws://` freely, directly contradicting the remit's MUST-use-TLS rule. A secondary theme is repo-local trust asymmetry: the tool deliberately prevents a checked-in `.env` from redirecting the endpoint or steering deploy state, but the same project `.env` can still supply the `LANGSMITH_API_KEY` that authenticates and targets the deploy. The remaining findings are hygiene-grade — MCP auth-header secrets passed on the argv, documentation advertising a `dev` command the code no longer wires, and unauthenticated/unpinned remote MCP servers in the repo's own `.mcp.json`.

rules.rule_text: The CLI reads a project's declared sources (`agent.json`, `AGENTS.md`, `tools.json`, `skills/`, `subagents/`), assembles the payload and managed-directory tree the platform expects, and upserts it — either creating a new managed agent or patching an existing one and syncing its Hub directory.
Validate that every MCP server URL referenced by the project is already registered in the workspace and invocable by the caller's identity — never auto-registering servers during deploy.
Offer a `--dry-run` that prints the exact payload and directory files it would send, without contacting any mutating endpoint.
Running the deployed agent itself, or performing any LLM inference — the CLI produces the artifact and hands it to the platform; it never invokes a model.
Default endpoint `https://api.smith.langchain.com`; scheme forced to HTTPS, userinfo rejected, proxy env ignored (`trust_env=False`).
Writing, committing to version control, logging, or printing platform / provider / Hub / tracing credentials or MCP auth-header values.
Folding secret material (e.g. `.env` contents) into the seeded bundle payload — environment stays environment; it is never merged into the skills, subagent, or managed-directory payload.
Pulling any file into the bundle from outside the resolved project root, via symlink, path traversal, or an undeclared source.
Letting repository-local deploy state steer authenticated requests (auth resolution ignores repo-local state).

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Deploying to an `agent_id` declared in `agent.json` that local state has not previously confirmed — the CLI must show the target agent name/id and require a `y/N` confirmation before updating that remote agent (bypassable only with `--yes`).

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Overwriting an existing project folder during `init` (requires `--force`).

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Deleting a workspace agent or MCP server (requires `y/N` confirmation or `--yes`).
Shipping a bundle assembled from anything other than the project's declared sources.
Deploying a project that failed configuration validation.
Reaching the platform over a non-HTTPS endpoint, or one carrying userinfo.
Bundling, registering, or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL (`http://` / `ws://`) — remote MCP server URLs MUST use TLS (`https` / `wss`).
Auto-registering an MCP server, or deploying while a referenced MCP server is unregistered or uninvocable by the caller's identity.
Creating a fresh agent via `--reset` while `agent.json` still declares an `agent_id`.
Writing, committing, logging, or printing credentials or MCP auth-header values.
HTTP 5xx responses are retried once with a short backoff; a directory-commit conflict (409/412) is re-fetched and re-applied once.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: The resolved endpoint is not HTTPS or carries userinfo, or `LANGSMITH_API_KEY` is missing — stop before any request.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: A project input is a symlink or escapes the project root — stop.

findings.summary: MCP server URLs are never validated as TLS — `mcp-servers add`/`update` and the deploy resolver accept `http://` and `ws://` schemes.
findings.description: The remit forbids registering or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL, but no code path checks the scheme: `_execute_mcp_server_add` only calls `urlparse` to derive a display name and passes the raw URL to `create_mcp_server`, and `resolve_referenced_servers` matches referenced URLs without inspecting their scheme. A cleartext MCP URL registered here — potentially carrying an `X-Api-Key` auth header — is later consumed by the platform-hosted agent over an interceptable channel. The CLI enforces HTTPS rigorously for its own platform endpoint yet applies no equivalent check on the parallel MCP trust path; if platform-side TLS enforcement is also absent this rises to Critical.
policy_rule_text: Bundling, registering, or deploying a project that references a remote MCP server over a non-TLS URL (`http://` / `ws://`) — remote MCP server URLs MUST use TLS (`https` / `wss`).

evidence.snippet: _execute_mcp_server_add uses urlparse(args.url).hostname only to name the server; args.url is passed to create_mcp_server with no https/wss scheme check (also mcp-servers update at :796).

_normalize_url lowercases and strips trailing slash but never inspects the scheme; resolve_referenced_servers matches http://-scheme URLs the same as https, so deploy proceeds against a non-TLS MCP server.

recommended_actions: In `_execute_mcp_server_add` and `_execute_mcp_server_update`, reject any `--url` whose scheme is not `https`/`wss` before calling `create_mcp_server`/`update_mcp_server`, mirroring `api_client._normalize_endpoint`.
Add a scheme check in `resolve_referenced_servers` so `deploy` halts if any referenced `mcp_server_url` is non-TLS, and add a unit test asserting rejection.

findings.summary: A checked-in project `.env` can supply `LANGSMITH_API_KEY`, steering which identity and workspace a deploy authenticates and ships to.
findings.description: The tool deliberately prevents a repo-local `.env` from steering authenticated requests — but only for the endpoint, proxy, and TLS keys, which are snapshotted and restored via `_PROJECT_DOTENV_BLOCKED_ENV_KEYS`. The API key itself is not on that blocklist, so a project `.env` discovered upward from the deploy directory populates `LANGSMITH_API_KEY` (when no shell value is set) and `ApiClient.from_env` authenticates with it, meaning a cloned hostile project could redirect the bundle to an attacker-controlled workspace. This is the same repo-local-steering risk the endpoint/state isolation exists to prevent, left open for the credential; scaffolded projects mitigate it by `.gitignore`-ing `.env`.
policy_rule_text: Letting repository-local deploy state steer authenticated requests (auth resolution ignores repo-local state).

evidence.snippet: _PROJECT_DOTENV_BLOCKED_ENV_KEYS covers LANGSMITH_ENDPOINT/proxy/SSL keys but not LANGSMITH_API_KEY/LANGCHAIN_API_KEY, so a project .env can set the auth key (loaded with override=False at :137).
from_env reads LANGSMITH_API_KEY / LANGCHAIN_API_KEY straight from os.environ after project .env load, with no check that the value did not originate from repo-local state.

recommended_actions: Either add the API-key env names to the project-`.env` blocklist (loading them only from shell env or `~/.deepagents/.env`), or emit a clear stderr warning when the key resolves from a project-local `.env`.

findings.summary: MCP auth-header secrets are passed as `--header KEY=VALUE` on the command line, exposing them to process listings and shell history.
findings.description: `mcp-servers add`/`update` take the auth header value directly from argv via `_parse_header_args`, and the README and post-init walkthrough model the pattern `--header X-Api-Key=$LANGSMITH_API_KEY`. Values on the command line are visible to any same-user process via `/proc/<pid>/cmdline` and are written to shell history, so a credential the CLI otherwise redacts (`***` on `get`) is exposed at the moment of registration. No environment-variable or stdin input path is offered as a safer alternative.

evidence.snippet: _parse_header_args splits each --header entry KEY=VALUE straight from argv; the value is the live secret, exposed in the process argument vector and shell history.
Documented usage `--header X-Api-Key=$LANGSMITH_API_KEY` places the resolved secret on the command line.

recommended_actions: Accept header values indirectly — e.g. `--header X-Api-Key` reading from a named env var or prompting on stdin — and document that as the preferred path, keeping the literal secret out of argv.

findings.summary: Shipped documentation advertises a `dev` command and a threat model for surfaces that no longer exist in this deploy-only package.
findings.description: `main._dispatch_command` wires only `init`, `deploy`, `agents`, and `mcp-servers`, yet the README's IMPORTANT banner still lists the surface as `init, dev, deploy`, and `THREAT_MODEL.md` — generated 2026-03-28 before the 0.1.0 REPL split — describes the interactive TUI, MCP loader, sandbox, and unauthenticated dev server as in-scope even though its own banner states those moved to `libs/code`. Operators reading the shipped docs get a materially wrong picture of the tool's attack surface and command set.

evidence.snippet: _dispatch_command handles init/deploy/agents/mcp-servers and redirects everything else; no `dev` command exists, contradicting the README banner listing "init, dev, deploy".
Banner admits the doc predates the 0.1.0 split and its REPL/MCP/sandbox/dev-server sections "no longer apply", yet those threats (T1-T10) remain the body of the shipped threat model.

recommended_actions: Remove `dev` from the README surface description (or wire/redirect the command explicitly), and either regenerate `THREAT_MODEL.md` for the deploy-only package or replace it with a pointer to `libs/code/THREAT_MODEL.md`.

findings.summary: The repo's root `.mcp.json` registers two remote MCP servers with no authentication and no version/integrity pinning.
findings.description: The MCP minimum-bar evaluation of the in-scope root `.mcp.json` finds two remote `http`-type servers (`docs-langchain`, `reference-langchain`) that pass the TLS check (both `https`) but declare no client authentication and no mechanism to pin or verify the tool definitions they serve. Their responses enter the repo's dev-time agent context, so a compromise or silent rug-pull of either endpoint could inject tampered tool descriptions; the exposure is bounded by their being read-only public documentation services.

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned

evidence.snippet: Two entries under mcpServers (docs-langchain, reference-langchain) are type http over https with no auth field and no version pin or integrity reference.

recommended_actions: Confirm both endpoints are first-party LangChain services and, where the platform supports it, pin/verify the served tool set or fingerprint the config so a silent change is detected.

positives.title: HTTPS-only platform transport with hardened client
positives.description: The endpoint is forced to HTTPS with embedded userinfo rejected (`_normalize_endpoint`), and the `httpx` client sets `trust_env=False` so proxy and TLS-cert environment variables cannot redirect or downgrade the connection.

positives.title: Bundle path containment — symlink and traversal rejection
positives.description: Every project input is checked against symlinks and resolved-path containment within the project root before it is read, so no file outside the declared project can be folded into the bundle.

positives.title: Pure-function bundle assembled only from declared sources
positives.description: The payload and managed-directory tree are built by a pure function over the parsed project, emitting only managed paths (`AGENTS.md`, `tools.json`, `skills/`, `subagents/`); `.env` and other undeclared content are never merged in.

positives.title: Deploy-target and destructive-action confirmation gates
positives.description: Deploying to an `agent_id` declared in `agent.json` that local state has not confirmed prompts y/N before updating the remote agent, agent/MCP deletions confirm, and `--reset` with a declared `agent_id` is refused.

positives.title: MCP resolution never auto-registers and ignores stale cache
positives.description: The deploy resolver always queries the live workspace MCP list, verifies `can_invoke`, and refuses to proceed on any unresolved or uninvocable server rather than auto-registering it.

positives.title: Repo-local state and endpoint isolation from authenticated requests
positives.description: Deploy state lives under `~/.deepagents/` keyed by project root plus endpoint, and a project `.env` is blocked from setting endpoint/proxy/TLS keys, so a checked-in repo cannot silently redirect where a deploy is sent.

positives.title: MCP auth-header redaction and committed pinned lockfile
positives.description: `mcp-servers get` redacts header values to `***`, and a committed `uv.lock` pins every dependency by sha256 with CI lockfile checks and Dependabot active.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files were found and no log handler is installed at runtime; the CLI's `logging` calls have no sink, so this absence is itself the basis for the Monitor Continuously score of 1 (no standalone finding is filed, per the inferred-log convention).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The tool has genuinely strong, operative controls on its core promises — HTTPS-only transport, bundle path-containment, deterministic confirmation gates, and MCP resolution that never auto-registers — which lift Limit Your Domain, Zero Trust, and Supply Chain into the Established range. It is pulled down by two things the framework does not do: it keeps no durable action log (logging calls exist but no handler is installed, so mutating deploys and deletions leave only transient stdout), and its adversarial-testing evidence is a stale auto-generated threat model plus security-flavored unit tests rather than an active red team. The net posture is a well-engineered tool with a real MCP-transport gap and thin runtime auditability.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The surface is architecturally minimal — four fixed `argparse` subcommands, no LLM and no general-purpose capability, and a bare invocation that redirects to the separate `deepagents-code` package (`main._dispatch_command`); the only blemish is documentation still advertising a `dev` command the code no longer wires.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: There is no model or knowledge base inside the CLI to poison; it consumes only the project's own declared sources and the trusted workspace MCP list, and data minimization is sound — `.env` contents are never folded into the bundle (`payload.build_directory_files` assembles only managed paths).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Operative controls are real and on-path — symlink/path-escape rejection (`project._ensure_project_contained`), HTTPS-plus-userinfo enforcement with `trust_env=False` (`api_client._normalize_endpoint`), and confirmation gates on remote-agent update and deletes — but the MCP URL scheme is never validated (PRAX-2026-07-17-001) and a project-local `.env` can still supply the auth key (PRAX-2026-07-17-002).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A committed `uv.lock` pins every dependency with a sha256 hash and CI enforces lockfile/SDK-pin checks with `dependabot.yml` active, though abstract dependency floors use `>=` (with `langsmith>=0.9.3` carrying no upper bound) and the repo's own `.mcp.json` servers are unpinned and unauthenticated (PRAX-2026-07-17-005).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: There is documented threat modeling and security-focused unit tests that exercise the containment controls (non-HTTPS/userinfo rejection in `test_api_client.py`, symlink rejection in `test_project.py`), but the shipped `THREAT_MODEL.md` is stale and out-of-scope for this package and there is no evidence of an ongoing adversarial exercise that changed the design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Logging infrastructure exists in source (`logging.getLogger`, `logger.debug/warning`) but no handler is installed — the code comment in `config.py` notes `logger.warning` output is invisible to users — so mutating operations (deploy PATCH, agent/MCP deletion) leave no durable, structured record beyond transient stdout summaries.
